sections.description: A relative clause connects two ideas about the same person, place or thing without writing two separate sentences. We introduce these clauses with a relative pronoun.

sections.title: Use
items: who → refers to people: The student **who** got the highest mark received a prize.
which → refers to things and animals: The jacket **which** I bought on sale is already broken.
where → refers to places: That's the park **where** we used to play as children.
whose → shows who something belongs to: I met a woman **whose** son plays in a famous band.

sections.title: Be careful
items: We can use who for a pet or animal when we treat it as a personality: My cat, who's called Luna, sleeps all day.
After the relative pronoun, do not repeat the subject or object pronoun.
✓ She's the woman **who** designed this building.
✓ Here's the report **which** you asked for.

sections.description: A non-defining relative clause adds bonus information about someone or something. The main sentence is still completely clear and correct without it.

sections.title: Use
items: To add extra detail that is not essential to identifying the noun: My grandfather, **who** turned 80 last year, still goes jogging every morning.

sections.title: Useful notes
items: Non-defining relative clauses are always separated from the main clause by commas.
Our history teacher, **whose** books are always fascinating, won an award last month.

sections.description:
A defining relative clause identifies exactly which person, thing or place we are talking about. Without it, the sentence loses its meaning or becomes unclear.
Imagine there are several students in a classroom. Only one of them has a red backpack.
*The student **who** has the red backpack is my best friend.*
If we take out the relative clause, we have no idea which student is meant.

sections.title: Use
items: To specify which person or thing we mean: The phone **which** rang during the lesson belonged to a student.

sections.title: Useful notes
items: We do not use commas with defining relative clauses.
In defining relative clauses, *that* can replace *who* or *which*.
✓ The doctor *who* treated me was very kind.
✓ The doctor *that* treated me was very kind.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Complete with a relative pronoun
exercises.title: A: Complete with a relative pronoun
exercises.instructions: Write who, which, where or whose in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write who, which, where or whose in each gap.

items.sentence: Do you remember the man ...... helped us at the station?
This is the camera ...... I used on holiday.
That's the library ...... I study after school.
I have a friend ...... mother works at the hospital.
The doctor ...... examined me was very friendly.
Is this the song ...... you heard on the radio?
They stayed in a hotel ...... the rooms had sea views.
items.explanation: **A hotel** = place → **where** the rooms had views.

items.sentence: The girl ...... bag was lost looked very upset.

exercises.title: B: Defining or non-defining
exercises.instructions: Add commas where needed. If no commas are needed, write 'No commas'.
exercises.studentInstruction: Add commas where needed. If no commas are needed, write 'No commas'.

items.sentence: My brother who lives in Manchester is a chef.
items.answer: My brother, who lives in Manchester, is a chef.
acceptedAnswers: My brother, who lives in Manchester, is a chef.
items.explanation: Extra information about **my brother** → non-defining → commas.

items.sentence: The bike which is outside the shop is mine.
items.explanation: Identifies which bike → defining clause → **no commas**.

items.sentence: Mrs Carter who teaches history is retiring soon.
items.answer: Mrs Carter, who teaches history, is retiring soon.
acceptedAnswers: Mrs Carter, who teaches history, is retiring soon.
items.explanation: Extra information about **Mrs Carter** → non-defining → commas.

items.sentence: The people who arrived late missed the introduction.
This castle which was built in the 12th century is open to visitors.
items.answer: This castle, which was built in the 12th century, is open to visitors.
acceptedAnswers: This castle, which was built in the 12th century, is open to visitors.
items.explanation: Extra information about the castle → non-defining → commas.

items.sentence: Students who forget their homework must speak to the teacher.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Use who, which or that
exercises.title: C: Use who, which or that
exercises.instructions: Choose the best option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the best option in each sentence.

items.originalSentence: He's the neighbour **who / which / where** looks after our dog.
items.completedSentence: He's the neighbour who looks after our dog.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "who" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "who" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "which" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.which: "which" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("looks after our dog.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: This is the programme **who / that / where** everyone watched last night.
items.completedSentence: This is the programme that everyone watched last night.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "that" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "that" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "who" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.who: "who" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("everyone watched last night.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Do you know a restaurant **that / who / whose** serves vegetarian food?
items.completedSentence: Do you know a restaurant that serves vegetarian food?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "that" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "that" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "who" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.who: "who" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("serves vegetarian food?").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: wallet was stolen called the police.
items.originalSentence: The man **which / whose / where** wallet was stolen called the police.
items.completedSentence: The man whose wallet was stolen called the police.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "whose" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "whose" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "which" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.which: "which" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("wallet was stolen called the police.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: That's the village **who / where / whose** my dad grew up.
items.completedSentence: That's the village where my dad grew up.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "where" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "where" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "who" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.who: "who" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("my dad grew up.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: The tablet **who / which / whose** I ordered arrived yesterday.
items.completedSentence: The tablet which I ordered arrived yesterday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "which" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "which" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "who" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.who: "who" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("I ordered arrived yesterday.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: She's the singer **which / that / where** won the competition.
items.completedSentence: She's the singer that won the competition.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "that" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "that" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "which" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.which: "which" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("won the competition.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: We visited the gallery **where / whose / which** has modern paintings.
items.completedSentence: We visited the gallery which has modern paintings.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "which" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "which" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "where" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.where: "where" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("has modern paintings.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: D: Join the sentences
exercises.instructions: Rewrite each pair as one sentence using a relative clause.
exercises.studentInstruction: Rewrite each pair as one sentence using a relative clause.

items.displayPrompt: I know a boy. He can solve a Rubik's cube very quickly.

cues: I know a boy. He can solve a Rubik's cube very quickly.

items.answer: I know a boy who can solve a Rubik's cube very quickly.
acceptedAnswers: I know a boy who can solve a Rubik's cube very quickly.
items.explanation: Join with **who** for a person → **who can solve**.

items.displayPrompt: We rented a flat. It was close to the station.

cues: We rented a flat. It was close to the station.

items.answer: We rented a flat which was close to the station.
acceptedAnswers: We rented a flat which was close to the station.
items.explanation: Join with **which** for a thing → **which was close**.

items.displayPrompt: That's the girl. Her sister won the prize.

cues: That's the girl. Her sister won the prize.

items.answer: That's the girl whose sister won the prize.
acceptedAnswers: That's the girl whose sister won the prize.

items.displayPrompt: This is the school. My mother studied there.

cues: This is the school. My mother studied there.

items.answer: This is the school where my mother studied.
acceptedAnswers: This is the school where my mother studied.

items.displayPrompt: I found a wallet. It was under the table.

cues: I found a wallet. It was under the table.

items.answer: I found a wallet which was under the table.
acceptedAnswers: I found a wallet which was under the table.

items.displayPrompt: The café is very popular. We met there on Saturday.

cues: The café is very popular. We met there on Saturday.

items.answer: The café where we met on Saturday is very popular.
acceptedAnswers: The café where we met on Saturday is very popular.

exercises.title: E: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one mistake with relative clauses. Find the error and write the whole sentence correctly.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one mistake with relative clauses. Find the error and write the whole sentence correctly.

items.displayPrompt: That's the girl who **she** won the art prize.

cues: That's the girl who **she** won the art prize.

items.answer: That's the girl who won the art prize.
acceptedAnswers: That's the girl who won the art prize.

items.displayPrompt: This is the bag which I bought **it** yesterday.

cues: This is the bag which I bought **it** yesterday.

items.answer: This is the bag which I bought yesterday.
acceptedAnswers: This is the bag which I bought yesterday.

items.displayPrompt: My cousin, who **he** works in Berlin, is coming home.

cues: My cousin, who **he** works in Berlin, is coming home.

items.answer: My cousin, who works in Berlin, is coming home.
acceptedAnswers: My cousin, who works in Berlin, is coming home.
items.explanation: Remove extra **he** in non-defining clause → **who works**.

items.displayPrompt: The film **who** we watched was really funny.

cues: The film **who** we watched was really funny.

items.answer: The film which we watched was really funny.
acceptedAnswers: The film which we watched was really funny.

items.displayPrompt: The house where I lived **there** is near the river.

cues: The house where I lived **there** is near the river.

items.answer: The house where I lived is near the river.
acceptedAnswers: The house where I lived is near the river.

items.displayPrompt: My neighbour **that he** plays the drums is very noisy.

cues: My neighbour **that he** plays the drums is very noisy.

items.answer: My neighbour who plays the drums is very noisy.
acceptedAnswers: My neighbour who plays the drums is very noisy.

exercises.title: F: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word in bold.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word in bold.

items.sentence:
The woman is a doctor. Her daughter is in my class. **WHOSE**
The woman ...... daughter is in my class is a doctor.
items.explanation: Possession → woman **whose** daughter is in my class.

items.sentence:
This is the shop. I bought my jacket there. **WHERE**
This is the shop ...... I bought my jacket.
items.explanation: Place → shop **where** I bought my jacket.

items.sentence:
I found a phone. It belongs to Sam. **WHICH**
I found a phone ...... belongs to Sam.

The man fixed our car. I spoke to him yesterday. **WHO**
The man ...... I spoke to yesterday fixed our car.

Do you know the reason? They cancelled the trip for that reason. **WHY**
Do you know the reason ...... they cancelled the trip?

This is the beach. We learned to surf here. **WHERE**
This is the beach ...... we learned to surf.
items.explanation: Place → beach **where** we learned to surf.

items.sentenceAfter: opened the door was my aunt.

items.explanation: Possession → dog **whose** owner lives next door.

items.sentenceAfter: I bought yesterday is too small.

items.explanation: Possession → boy **whose** father is a firefighter.

exercises.title: H: Complete the text
exercises.instructions: Write one relative word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one relative word in each gap.

items.sentence: I know a teacher ...... works in Edinburgh.
She teaches at a school ...... has students from many countries.
The school, ...... is near the city centre, is very old.
Her colleague is a man ...... lessons are always exciting.
The classroom ...... they study has a large screen.
She explained the reason ...... she became a teacher.
items.explanation: Reason → reason **why** she became a teacher.

items.sentence: She has students ...... speak more than two languages.
It's the kind of school ...... I would love to visit.
items.explanation: **A school** = place → **where** I would love to visit.

exercises.title: I: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence:
I know a musician. She plays in an orchestra.
I know a musician ...... in an orchestra.
items.explanation: Combine: musician **who plays** in an orchestra.

items.sentence:
The scarf was beautiful. I bought it at the market.
The scarf ...... at the market was beautiful.
items.explanation: Omit the object: scarf **I bought** at the market.

items.sentence:
There's a shop near here. It sells handmade gifts.
There's a shop ...... handmade gifts near here.

She is a painter. Her latest picture won a prize.
She is a painter ...... a prize.
items.explanation: Possession → painter **whose picture won** a prize.

items.sentence:
The village was peaceful. We visited it in June.
The village ...... in June was peaceful.
items.explanation: Omit the object: village **we visited** in June.

items.sentence:
He works with a doctor. She studies tropical diseases.
He works with a doctor ...... tropical diseases.
